project.name: starshell
project.description: A minimal, fast shell prompt with a fixed, hand-picked set of modules, built for Linux only.
project.relationship_to_upstream: starshell is a standalone fork of Starship (starship.rs). It has its own version history and is not mechanically synced with upstream Starship releases. Upstream is used only as a manual reference when the maintainer notices a change worth porting into the core engine or into a module this project still uses.
project.distribution: Binary only. No install script, no OS packages, no docs site, no CI/CD pipeline, no crates.io publishing.

repository_layout.build.rs: build script, at repo root, generates version/build metadata only
repository_layout.src/: all source code, directly under repo root

consequences: No Windows-specific dependencies (deelevate, windows crate, winres) or code paths (modules/utils/directory_win.rs does not exist).
nix crate is a real, load-bearing dependency: used by modules/utils/directory_nix.rs and by git_status.rs's WSL-detection path.
All shell-init scripts are still shipped regardless of OS, because shell choice is a runtime CLI argument (`starshell init <shell>`), not a compile-time platform branch -- see init_system below.

core_engine.definition: Everything in src/ except modules/*.rs and configs/*.rs. This is the part of the codebase that changes for reasons unrelated to which modules exist: CLI behavior, config parsing, prompt assembly, formatting, git/repo discovery infrastructure.

files.main.rs: Binary entry point. Defines the CLI (clap-based): subcommands for prompt rendering, module introspection, shell init/completions, config editing, config-schema output, bug-report. Imports the library crate as `starshell::...`.
files.lib.rs: Library crate root. Declares all top-level modules, invokes shadow_rs's `shadow!` macro for build metadata (PKG_VERSION, CLAP_LONG_VERSION), and exposes thread-count helpers driven by the STARSHIP_NUM_THREADS env var.
files.config.rs: Loads and parses starship.toml. Exposes get_custom_modules() for reading the [custom.*] table, and generic get_config()/get_module_config() lookups used throughout.
files.context.rs: The Context struct: current directory, environment snapshot, shell/target info, and the core git repo-discovery machinery (gix-based Repo/get_repo(), shared by directory.rs, custom.rs's require_repo option, and every git_* module). Also owns the generic detect_env_vars/detect_env_vars2 mechanism used by username and python to conditionally activate based on environment variables being present -- this is unrelated to any module named 'env_var'; no such module exists in this project.
files.module.rs: Defines ALL_MODULES (the authoritative list of statically-dispatched module names) and the Module struct/segment-rendering primitives.
files.modules/mod.rs: Dispatches a module name to its implementation via handle(), and supplies one-line descriptions via description(). custom is dispatched dynamically by "custom." prefix match, not by exact name, so it does not appear in ALL_MODULES.
files.configs/mod.rs: Defines FullConfig, the aggregate struct combining the root prompt config with every module's config struct. Drives starship.toml parsing and (optionally) JSON-schema generation.
files.configs/starship_root.rs: Defines StarshipRootConfig (format, right_format, palettes, profiles, etc.) and PROMPT_ORDER, the default module rendering order used only when a user's format string is "$all".
files.print.rs: Assembles the final prompt string: resolves the format string against PROMPT_ORDER or an explicit format, expands "$custom" into every configured [custom.*] entry via handle_module()/should_add_implicit_module(), and renders styled segments.
files.configure.rs: Implements `starshell config` (opening the config file in $EDITOR) and related config-editing subcommands.
files.formatter/: The format-string parser and renderer. spec.pest defines the grammar; string_formatter.rs and parser.rs implement it; version.rs provides shared version-string formatting used by python (via versions crate) and time.
files.init/: Shell integration. init/mod.rs unconditionally embeds every script in this directory via include_str!() and dispatches by the CompletionShell enum / Commands::Init match arms in main.rs -- see init_system.critical_invariant below.
files.utils/: Command execution (create_command, exec_cmd, mock_cmd for tests), custom serde deserializer enforcing the allowed top-level TOML key set, path/statusline helpers.
files.segment.rs: Styled text segment primitive used by every module's rendering output.
files.bug_report.rs: Implements `starshell bug-report`; the only consumer of the urlencoding crate and one of several consumers of os_info (for OS/version info in the report body, independent of whether the os module is enabled).

module_wiring_contract.description: A statically-dispatched module must be registered consistently in exactly four places, all agreeing on the same string name. custom is the one exception: it is dispatched dynamically by prefix match and does not appear in ALL_MODULES or PROMPT_ORDER's module-name test, though it does appear in PROMPT_ORDER for default-ordering purposes and has its own field-shape (an IndexMap, not a single struct) in FullConfig.

points.must_be: Alphabetically sorted (enforced by a test in this file). Contains every statically-dispatched module name.

points.construct: mod <name>; + match arm in handle() + entry in description()
points.must_be: One line per point per module, kept alphabetical by convention (not enforced by a compiler test beyond description() coverage).
A field whose type is the module's Config struct (or an IndexMap<String, Config> for dynamically-named instances like custom).
Only affects default rendering when format = "$all". A module can be fully wired and functional while absent from this list; it just won't appear under the default format.

compiler_enforced_checks: module.rs: test_all_modules_is_in_alphabetical_order -- fails if ALL_MODULES drifts from sorted order.
configs/mod.rs: test_all_modules_in_full_config -- fails if any name in ALL_MODULES is missing as a key in FullConfig's serialized form.
module_wiring_contract.not_compiler_enforced: Nothing checks that PROMPT_ORDER matches ALL_MODULES, or that modules/mod.rs's description() entries match ALL_MODULES exactly (the all_modules_have_description test only checks descriptions aren't the placeholder string, not that every ALL_MODULES entry has a *custom* one).

list.name: directory
list.purpose: Displays the current working directory, with truncation and path substitution rules.

list.name: git_branch
depends_on: modules::git_status::uses_reftables (direct cross-module import -- git_status must exist for this module to compile)

list.name: git_commit
list.purpose: Displays the active commit hash / tag.

list.name: git_state
list.purpose: Displays the current git operation in progress (rebase, merge, etc.) and its step.

list.name: git_status
list.purpose: Displays working-tree status counts (staged, modified, untracked, ahead/behind, etc.). Declared `pub` (not just `mod`) in modules/mod.rs specifically so git_branch.rs can import uses_reftables from it.

list.name: time
list.purpose: Displays the current local time in a configurable format.

list.name: character
list.purpose: Displays the prompt character/arrow, styled by last command success/failure.

list.name: os
list.purpose: Displays an icon (and optionally name/version/codename) for the detected OS or Linux distribution. Disabled by default; must be explicitly enabled with `disabled = false` and referenced in a format string to render anything. Ships a built-in symbol table covering ~60 distros/platforms recognized by os_info, overridable per-entry in [os.symbols].
depends_on: os_info crate (already required independently by bug_report.rs)

list.name: python
list.purpose: Displays the active Python version / virtualenv name.

list.name: line_break
list.purpose: Inserts a line break in multi-line prompt formats.

list.name: custom
list.dispatch: dynamic ("custom.<name>" prefix match; also expands bare "$custom" in a format string to every configured [custom.*] entry via print.rs's handle_module/should_add_implicit_module)
list.purpose: Runs a user-specified shell command and displays its output as a segment. Supports gating by detected files/extensions/folders, an arbitrary `when` shell condition, `require_repo`, and OS filtering. The general-purpose mechanism for displaying anything not covered by a dedicated module.

not_present.description: Modules from upstream Starship that do not exist in this codebase at all -- no file, no wiring, nothing to remove or disable. Re-adding any of these means copying the module/config file pair from an upstream checkout and wiring all four points above, then verifying no other file (grep the whole tree, not just the module's own filename) has orphaned helper functions whose only purpose was serving that module.

examples_considered_and_intentionally_ex.name: env_var
examples_considered_and_intentionally_ex.reason: Displayed the value of a named environment variable via an env_var.<name> table. Removed after python and username's existing detect_env_vars/detect_env_vars2 activation mechanism was judged sufficient and custom was judged capable of covering any remaining ad-hoc display need.

examples_considered_and_intentionally_ex.name: fill
examples_considered_and_intentionally_ex.reason: Prompt-formatting pad-character glue; not depended on by any module in this list.

examples_considered_and_intentionally_ex.name: shell
examples_considered_and_intentionally_ex.reason: Displays detected shell name; not depended on by any module in this list.

examples_considered_and_intentionally_ex.name: status
examples_considered_and_intentionally_ex.reason: Displays last command exit code; not depended on by any module in this list.

examples_considered_and_intentionally_ex.name: cmd_duration
examples_considered_and_intentionally_ex.reason: Displays command elapsed time. Its removal is also why notify-rust and two small formatting helpers (elapsed-time humanizer, integer humanizer) do not exist in utils/mod.rs.

examples_considered_and_intentionally_ex.name: vcs
examples_considered_and_intentionally_ex.reason: Meta-dispatcher to whichever VCS is detected; not depended on by any module in this list, and nothing in this list depends on it either.

not_present.everything_else: All other upstream modules (toolchain/language version modules, cloud-provider modules, container/orchestration modules, etc.) are simply absent -- not disabled, not stubbed, no trace in the wiring files.

dependency_map.description: Each entry lists every file in src/ that references the crate, verified by direct grep against the current tree. A crate with a single consumer is a strong signal that removing that consumer should prompt removing the crate too.

nu-ansi-term: and most modules for styled output
schemars: config.rs, every configs/*.rs, print.rs -- optional, gated behind the config-schema feature
serde / serde_json: config.rs, every configs/*.rs, utils/serde.rs, utils/statusline.rs, print.rs
shadow-rs: lib.rs (via the shadow! macro, not path syntax) -- provides PKG_VERSION/CLAP_LONG_VERSION; build.rs also depends on it as a build-dependency
toml / toml_edit: config.rs, configure.rs, most modules' tests, configs/mod.rs

not_present.description: Crates absent from Cargo.toml because nothing in this tree references them. Re-adding a module that needs one of these means re-adding the crate too.

init_system.description: Shell integration for bash, zsh, fish, elvish, nushell, tcsh, xonsh, powershell, and ion. Each shell's init snippet is a static file in src/init/, embedded into the binary at compile time.
init_system.critical_invariant: src/init/mod.rs includes every one of these files via include_str!() completely unconditionally -- there is no #[cfg] gate keyed on platform or shell choice. Shell selection happens at runtime via `starshell init <shell>`, dispatched through the CompletionShell enum and Commands::Init match arms in main.rs, not through compile-time branching. Deleting any file this module includes breaks the build on every platform, regardless of whether that shell 'belongs' to the platform you're targeting. To drop support for a shell, you must remove it from three places in lockstep: the CompletionShell enum, the relevant match arms in main.rs, and the include_str!/const declaration in init/mod.rs -- deleting only the script file is not sufficient and will not compile.

runtime_protocol.description: User-facing names and file conventions, deliberately kept identical to upstream Starship for drop-in compatibility with existing shell configurations, independent of the starshell package/binary rename.
runtime_protocol.config_file: starship.toml, located via the STARSHIP_CONFIG environment variable or the platform default config path.
environment_variables: and others read via context.rs's generic env-var accessors
runtime_protocol.cli_program_name: starshell (the compiled binary name and the name embedded into generated shell completions -- this one detail was changed from upstream, everything else in this section was not)

cargo_toml.package_identity: name = starshell, version = 0.1.0. No documentation/homepage/repository/include/readme/categories/keywords fields -- these only matter for `cargo package`/crates.io publishing, which this project never does.

features.default: empty -- no features enabled by default
features.config-schema: optional, gated on the schemars dependency; enables `starshell config-schema` to emit a JSON schema for FullConfig

release_profile.rationale: Tuned for a binary built rarely but executed on every shell prompt render: maximize runtime speed and minimize size at the cost of build time. panic=abort applies only to this profile; `cargo test` builds under a separate profile and retains normal unwinding.

known_footguns.risk: Assuming a shell's init script can be deleted because its target platform doesn't match your build target. See init_system.critical_invariant -- include_str! calls in init/mod.rs are unconditional.

Deleting a module's own two files (modules/<name>.rs, configs/<name>.rs) and its four wiring points is necessary but not sufficient. Other files may contain helper functions, test fixtures, or allowlist entries that existed solely to support that module (precedent: a dead test-fixture table in utils/mod.rs, a dead helper function in config.rs, a stale entry in utils/serde.rs's allowed-top-level-key check, all discovered only by grepping for the module's name/type across the whole tree, not just checking whether its own two files still compile).

A module's Cargo.toml dependency may already be present for an unrelated reason (os_info was already required by bug_report.rs before the os module was re-added). Check the dependency_map before assuming a crate needs to be added; check it again before assuming one should be removed.

An #[allow(dead_code)] does not mean the code is legitimately used elsewhere for a reason clippy can't see -- it can just as easily mean a function has been orphaned and nobody removed the allow along with its last caller. Precedent: utils/mod.rs had a #[cfg(test)]/#[cfg(not(test))] pair of context_path() functions, each individually marked #[allow(dead_code)], with zero callers anywhere in the tree (test or production) once verified by grep. The allow had been silently hiding this since it predates this fork. Do not treat the presence of #[allow(dead_code)] as evidence the code is needed -- grep for real callers before trusting it, the same way you would for any other function.

Before trusting the tree, grep for every #[allow(...)], #![allow(...)], and #[warn(...)] site and check each individually -- do not assume a suppression is still contextually valid just because it was valid when written. Most in this codebase are legitimate and narrowly scoped (test-only struct fields with no reader by design, generated-code lint false positives from pest_derive, platform-conditional numeric conversions with an inline comment explaining why). One category is worth extra scrutiny every time: any #[allow(dead_code)] on a pub fn, since that is exactly the lint that would otherwise catch an orphaned function left over from a deleted module or a refactor. The one legitimate exception to the disallowed-methods ban on std::process::Command::new lives in utils/mod.rs's create_command() -- confirm it remains the *only* call site (grep 'Command::new') if you ever touch process-spawning code, since that single exception is what makes the project-wide ban in clippy.toml meaningful.
